coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of nano-hybrid polymethacrylate-carbon dots as pour point depressant and combined with ethylene-vinyl acetate resin to improve the cold flow properties of diesel fuels
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiaokang
Li, Nana
Wei, Zhong
Han, Sheng
Dai, Bin
Lin, Hualin
coredata.dc:description: The application of nanoparticles in terms of improving the cold flow properties of diesel fuels attracted more and more attention, and the composite technology of pour point depressant could further improve the cold flow properties of diesel fuels. A new type of nano-hybrid pour point depressant (PPD) was created in this study by copolymerising surface-modified carbon dots with tetradecyl methacrylate, which demonstrated good cold flow properties for diesel fuels (ΔSP = 21 °C, ΔCFPP = 9 °C). Besides, the ethylene-vinyl acetate (EVA) resin was also tested as a novel class of PPD to improve the cold flow properties of diesel fuels and exhibited excellent effect (ΔCFPP = 13 °C) on reducing the cold filter plugging point of diesel fuels. Thus, the combined technology of these two type of PPDs was used to further improve the cold flow properties of diesel fuels. The results indicated that the composite PPDs showed a great decrease in both solid point and cold filter plugging point of diesel fuels at a dose of 1500 ppm (ΔSP = 30 °C, ΔCFPP = 20 °C), which indicates better performance than achieved by either of them alone. In addition, polynomial and quadratic function fitting models are established to describe how the two main factors affect the cold flow properties of diesel fuels, and the fitting effects are well. In the end, using a differential scanning calorimeter, polarised optical microscope and rheological analysis, the paraffin crystallisation and morphology of wax crystals for diesel fuels before and after treatment with PPDs were studied, and the mechanism of heterogeneous nucleation and crystallisation of paraffin induced by PPD was proposed to explain the effects of nano-hybrid PPD, EVA resin and combined PPDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
pour point depressant
Cold flow properties
Nano-hybrid
Crystallisation behaviour
Diesel fuels

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of surface modified carbon dots
Preparation of carbon dots-PMA nanohybrid PPDs
Characterization of carbon dots and PPDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project Number 22008155 , 22075183 , 21878188 and 21975161 ), Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality Project (Project Number 18090503800 ), Shuguang Program of Shanghai Education Development Foundation and Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (Project Number 18SG52 ), Chenguang Program of Shanghai Education Development Foundation and Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (Project Number 19CG69 ), the Talent Development Foundation of Shanghai (Project Number 2018034 ), Shanghai Gaofeng & Gaoyuan Project for University Academic Program Development .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of synthetic carbon dots (a) and surface modification (b).
Schematic diagram of synthetic PMA-CD pour point depressant.
TEM images and Zeta potential of carbon dots before (a) and after (b) surface modification.
FT-IR spectra of CDs before and after surface modification.
FT-IR spectra of EVA resin with different VA content: (a) EVA40, (b) EVA25, (c) EVA18.
Depressive effects of the PMA-CDs nanohybrid PPDs with different dose and mass ratio.
3D response surface plot for CFPP and SP values affected by the dose and composition of PPDs.
Depressive effects of EVA resin with different VA content and dose.
Depressing effect of Combination of PMA-CD and EVA resin.
3D response surface plot for CFPP and SP values affected by the dose and composition of combined PPDs.

Polarised optical microscopy images at different temperatures: (a1–a4) pure diesel fuels, (b1–b4) diesel fuels treated with 1000 ppm of PMA14-CD0.5%, (c1–c4) diesel fuels treated with 1000 ppm of EVA25, (d1–d4) diesel fuels treated with 1000 ppm of PPD-2.
Differential scanning calorimetry thermogram during the cooling process of diesel fuels treated with different PPDs at 1000 ppm; Pure diesel fuels, EVA25, PMA14-CD0.5% and PDD-2.
Viscosity-temperature curves of pure diesel fuels treated with different PPDs at a dose of 1000 ppm; Pure diesel fuels, EVA25, PMA14-CD0.5% and PDD-2.
Effects of different brands of PPDs on diesel fuels at a dose of 1000 ppm.
Effects of three brands of PPDs on ΔSP and ΔCFPP of diesel fuels.
Possible mechanism of PMA14-CDs%, EVA25 and their combination for improving the cold flow properties of diesel fuels.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of nano-hybrid polymethacrylate-carbon dots as pour point depressant and combined with ethylene-vinyl acetate resin to improve the cold flow properties of diesel fuels

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shihezi University, North Fourth Road, Shihezi, Xinjiang 832003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shihezi University, North Fourth Road, Shihezi, Xinjiang 832003, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shanghai Institute of Technology, 100 Haiquan Road, Shanghai 201418, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shanghai Institute of Technology, 100 Haiquan Road, Shanghai 201418, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shihezi University, North Fourth Road, Shihezi, Xinjiang 832003, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The application of nanoparticles in terms of improving the cold flow properties of diesel fuels attracted more and more attention, and the composite technology of pour point depressant could further improve the cold flow properties of diesel fuels. A new type of nano-hybrid pour point depressant (PPD) was created in this study by copolymerising surface-modified carbon dots with tetradecyl methacrylate, which demonstrated good cold flow properties for diesel fuels (ΔSP = 21 °C, ΔCFPP = 9 °C). Besides, the ethylene-vinyl acetate (EVA) resin was also tested as a novel class of PPD to improve the cold flow properties of diesel fuels and exhibited excellent effect (ΔCFPP = 13 °C) on reducing the cold filter plugging point of diesel fuels. Thus, the combined technology of these two type of PPDs was used to further improve the cold flow properties of diesel fuels. The results indicated that the composite PPDs showed a great decrease in both solid point and cold filter plugging point of diesel fuels at a dose of 1500 ppm (ΔSP = 30 °C, ΔCFPP = 20 °C), which indicates better performance than achieved by either of them alone. In addition, polynomial and quadratic function fitting models are established to describe how the two main factors affect the cold flow properties of diesel fuels, and the fitting effects are well. In the end, using a differential scanning calorimeter, polarised optical microscope and rheological analysis, the paraffin crystallisation and morphology of wax crystals for diesel fuels before and after treatment with PPDs were studied, and the mechanism of heterogeneous nucleation and crystallisation of paraffin induced by PPD was proposed to explain the effects of nano-hybrid PPD, EVA resin and combined PPDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Diesel fuel, as an important fossil fuel, is mainly used in diesel engines, which are widely applied in many industries, such as transportation [1], construction and agriculture [2]. Diesel fuel is refined from crude oil, which mainly consists of long-chain n-alkanes with carbon numbers in the range of 12–22. The long-chain n-alkanes of diesel fuels tend to crystallize in cold areas with temperatures below zero, and then wax crystals continue to grow and form a three-dimensional (3D) network [3], which will block the standard filter in the engine pipe [4] and hinder diesel engine operation [5]. Some methods, such as winterization technique [6] or fractional winterization [7], blending with diesel [8] or crystallisation modifiers [9] and additives such as biomass additive [10], polyether [11] and polyethylene glycol ester [12] were used to improve the cold flow properties of diesel fuels, so as to ensure the normal operation of diesel engines in such cold areas. Among them, in view of their advantages on low cost and high efficiency, adding pour point depressants (PPDs) are considered one of the most cost-effective ways for both petrochemical oil [13] and biodiesel [14].

PPDs dissolved in diesel fuels can be eutectic with paraffin or adsorb on the surface of wax crystals during cooling process, to affect the precipitation [15], crystallisation [16] and wax crystal growth of paraffin [17]. Among many kinds of PPDs, polymeric PPDs have been widely studied and used because of their remarkable performance. Polymeric PPDs, polymers with specific branched chains of long-chain n-alkanes, mainly include polymethacrylate (eg. poly octadecyl acrylate [18] and poly octadecyl acrylate-co-maleic anhydride [19], ethylene-vinyl acetate (EVA) (eg. grafted EVA [20] and alcoholized EVA [21]), ethylene-butene copolymers [22], maleic anhydride polymers (eg. Poly (Styrene-co-Maleic Anhydride) [23], poly (maleic acid amide-co -vinyl acetate) [24], poly (styrene-alt-octadecyl maleimide) [25] and poly (fatty esters-co-succinic anhydride)) [26], and poly alpha-olefin used in biodiesel [27] or crude oil [28]. These PDDs could show an outstanding effect on improving the cold flow properties of diesel fuels, including reducing the solid point (SP) and cold filter plugging point (CFPP).

Colloidal ethylene-vinyl acetate copolymers as PPDs are widely used to improve the fluidity of diesel fuel in engines [29], the exploitation of crude oil [30] and the pipeline transportation of crude oil [31], and it is also a successful commercial PPD [32]. In addition, the depressant effect of EVA on diesel fuels is affected by the type and content of the EVA branch chain [29].

The PPDs based on polymethacrylate (PMA) are widely used in diesel fuels and crude oil. It is usually synthesized by copolymerization of high-carbon alkyl methacrylates with other molecules containing double bonds [33]. Modifying the branched-chain of PMA copolymers, e.g. changing the length of the branched carbon chain [34] or introducing polar groups such as benzene ring [35] and heterocycle [36], can further improve the cold flow properties of diesel fuels. The effect of high-carbon methacrylate with different carbon numbers on the PPD effect of diesel fuels was studied by Xu et al. [37], who found that tetradecanol had the best effect. Xie et al. [35] copolymerised tetradecyl methacrylate with benzyl methacrylate and achieve a good effect of coagulation and filtration reduction. Yang et al. [38] studied the PPD effect of a copolymer formed by nitrogenous polar monomer and tetradecyl methacrylate, indicating that ternary copolymerization of nitrogenous polymers could effectively improve the cold flow properties of diesel fuels. Although these PPDs can effectively improve the cold flow properties of diesel fuels, they are not an environmentally friendly or cost-effective option because they either contain too much nitrogen [38] or require massive additions [34]. Therefore, using fewer PPDs and reducing the content of nitrogen in PPDs as well as achieving the desired performance in improving the cold flow properties of diesel fuels is one of the urgent problems to be solved.

In recent years, the preparation of pour point depressant using nanomaterials including inorganic nanomaterials (montmorillonite [41], silicon dioxide [42], ferric oxide [43], etc.) and organic nanomaterials (graphene [44], carbon nanotubes [40], etc.) has attracted extensive attention. Due to their nano characteristics such as the extreme small size, good dispersion, large specific surface area and easy to modify, etc, nanomaterials not only can regulate the aggregation of copolymers, moreover, it but also prevent the accumulation and reduce the friction wax crystals as well as served as heterogeneous nucleation sites. Furthermore, the combination of nanomaterials and traditional PPDs can achieve better performance than using polymeric PPDs along in improve the rheological properties of crude oil and the cold flow properties of diesel fuels. These composite technologies usually include blending [45] and copolymerization [44], etc. Xue et al. [39] prepared nano silica-polymethacrylate PPDs by melt-blending method, and the nano-hybrid PPDs exhibited satisfactory reduction of CFPP and CP for diesel fuels (ΔSP = 18 °C, ΔCFPP = 6 °C). Zhao et al. [40] copolymerised surface modified graphene with tetradecyl methacrylate, and concluded that the improvement of cold flow properties of diesel fuels by adding graphene-tetradecyl nanocomposite PPD is better than that of polytetradecyl methacrylate. However, some nanomaterials, such as graphene and carbon nanotubes, are oversized to affect their dispersion in diesel fuels, and the colour of diesel fuels gains depth due to the dark colour of nanomaterials. Besides, some nanomaterials, such as montmorillonite and silicon dioxide, are inorganic non-metallic oxides that cannot burn or decompose and can deposit and damage the diesel fuels engine during combustion. Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimension materials, which have been widely studied and applied in many fields (e.g. bioscience, catalysis [46] and detection fields [47], etc). Carbon dots have many advantages, such as small size, excellent optical properties, good water solubility, low toxicity, environment-friendly [48], wide source of raw materials, low cost, good biocompatibility [49], etc. However, the application of CDs in diesel fuels is limited because of its poor oil solubility. At the same time, in view of the advantages of small size, easily modified surface and being used as heterogeneous nucleation sites, CDs have great potential as nanocomposite pour point depressant for diesel fuels. So far, few research has been conducted to improve the cold flow properties of diesel fuels through the use of nanocomposite PPDs in conjunction with CDs.

Based on the above, it can be inferred that the combination of nanomaterials and polymeric PPDs can further enhance the performance of PPDs, especially those formed by copolymerization of nano materials with monomers. This may be due to the linear structure of PPDs transform into micellar structure thourgh the copolymerization, which greatly enhances the heterogeneous nucleation ability of PPDs. At the same time, the combined technology between PPDs further increase the effect of pour point depressants on the cold flow properties of diesel fuels [29]. Thereby, in this study, a new type of oil-soluble carbon dots are synthesized and introduced to combine with the PMA-type PPDs by in-situ radical polymerisations for the first time. Namely, tetradecyl methacrylate is used to copolymerise CDs. The effect of the PMA-CDs nano-hybrid PPDs on the CFPP and SP of diesel fuels is better improved than the effect of pure PMA PPDs. Furthermore, the effect of a type of granular EVA resin on the cold flow properties of diesel fuels is studied, which shows a desirable performance in reducing the CFPP of diesel fuels. Subsequently, the combined technology of EVA resin and PMA-CDs PPDs is carried out, and the resulting complex PPDs achieve outstanding performance on the cold flow properties of diesel fuels. In addition, polynomial and quadratic function fitting models are established to describe how the two main factors affect cold flow properties of diesel fuels, and the fitting effect is satisfactory. Finally, the morphology of wax crystals and crystal growth of paraffin in diesel fuels are characterised using polarised optical microscopy (POM), differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and viscosity-temperature analysis to analyse the effect of the structure of nano-hybrid PPDs and synergistic effect with EVA resin on enhancing cold flow properties of diesel fuels. Furthermore, a mechanism for the heterogeneous nucleation and crystallisation behaviour of paraffin is proposed using PMA-CDs nano-hybrid PPDs and their combination with EVA resin.

The reagents required for this study are as follows: tetradecyl methacrylate, hexadecyl methacrylate, methacryloyl chloride, benzoyl peroxide, benzaldehyde, ethylenediamine, hydrochloric acid, toluene, absolute ethanol and EVA resin. They were all purchased from Titan Scientific Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Diesel fuels (0#) with the CFPP of −1 °C and SP of −14 °C was obtained from Shanghai Jinshan Gas Station, and more compositions and physicochemical properties of diesel fuels (0#) were provided in fig. S1(Support information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of surface modified carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Ethylenediamine (2 mL), benzaldehyde (6 mL) and hydrochloric acid (1 mL) were mixed and stirred for 20 min in ethanol (20 mL) at room temperature. The mixture was then transferred to a hydrothermal synthesis reactor and reacted at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, 100 ml of deionized water was added to the solvent to precipitate carbon quantum dots. The precipitates were collected by centrifugation, filtered and dried at 60 °C for 10 h. The dried product (1.5 g) was dissolved in 40 mL of DMF, then methacryloyl chloride (1 mL) and K2CO3 (3 g) were added to the solution and reacted under 40 °C for 5 h to graft double bonds on the surface of carbon quantum dots, then K2CO3 was removed and 80 ml of deionized water was added to precipitate carbon quantum dots. The sediments were collected by centrifugation. Finally, the product was dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 10 h. The schematic diagram of synthesis and modification of carbon dots was showed in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots-PMA nanohybrid PPDs
ce:section.ce:para: Tetradecyl methacrylate (C14MC, 3 g) and carbon dots with the mass ratio of 100:0100: 0.25, 100: 0.5, 100: 1, 100: 2 were mixed in 20 ml of toluene, and benzoyl peroxide as catalyst (1 wt %) was added. The mixture was stirred for 8 h at 110 °C under argon atmosphere. After reaction, the solution was evaporated, then the crude product (PMA14-CDx%) was washed with ethanol for several times, and dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 8 h. The products were named PMA14, PMA14-CD0.25%, PMA14-CD0.5%, PMA14-CD1% and PMA14-CD2%, respectively. The synthesis of PMA16-CDx% were performed similarly, starting from hexadecyl methacrylate (C16MC). The products were named PMA16, PMA16-CD0.25%, PMA16-CD0.5%, PMA16-CD1% and PMA16-CD2%, respectively. The preparation diagram of PMA-carbon dots nanohybrid pour point depressant was showed in Fig. 2 .

The microscopic morphologyof CDs was determined by transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL-2100, JAPAN). Zeta potentials of CDs were measured by a zeta potential and particle size analyzer (brook haven zetapals, USA). The functional groups and chemical bond of CDs, EVA resin and nano-hybrid PPDs were obtained by Nicolet iN10 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR) at a scanning range of 500 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1 for 32 scans and the 1H Nuclear Magnetic Resonance spectroscopy (NMR, -AVANCE III 500 MHz, Switzerland) by using deuterated chloroform (CDCl3) as the solvent. The cold flow property of diesel fuels, including solid point (SP) and cold filter plugging point (CFPP) was measured by the SYD-510F1 multifunctional low temperature tester (Shanghai Changji Instrument Co., Ltd., China), and the test method was carried out according to the requirements: ASTM D 6371 for CFPP and GB/T510 for SP. After adding PPDs, each sample of diesel fuels was ultrasonic treated for 15 min, and then tested three times. The rheological property of diesel fuels sample with the change of temperature was measured by HAAKE MARS III rheometer (Germany) at a constant shear rate of 5 S−1, and the test temperature range from 0 °C to −50 °C. The crystallisation behaviour and wax crystal morphology of diesel fuels samples with the change of temperature was observed by DM2500P polarizing optical microscopy (Leica, Germany) under a cooling rate of 0.8 °C/min from 0 °C to −45 °C, and microphotographs of all samples were taken at intervals of 5 °C and at a magnification of 100 × . The phase change temperature and enthalpy of diesel fuels during cooling process were measured by TA Q2000 Differential Scanning Calorimeter (TA Instruments), which each sample of 8–10 mg were cooled from 10 °C to - 50 °C at a scanning rate of 5 °C/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon dots and PPDs

ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 , It is clear seen from the TEM images that the particles are of spherical-like shape and disperse well. The average diameter of CDs ranged from 4 to 6 nm, and their size almost no change after surface modification. In addition, the surface potential of CDs were measured before and after surface modification, and the potential changed from +7.85 mV before modification to – 15.28 mV after modification. The amine group usually exhibites positive electricity while the carbon-carbon double bond and amide group usually show negative electricity. Thus, it can be inferred that methacrylate is successfully modified to the surface of carbon dots.

As shown in Fig. 4 , The curves of both unmodified and surface modified of CDs show the peaks at 1400 and 1640 cm−1, representing the carbon nitrogen bond (C–N stretching vibration peak) and carbon-carbon double bond in benzene, respectively, which is consistent with the raw materials of ethylenediamine and benzaldehyde. In addition, the curve of surface modified CDs shows extra peaks at 2972, 1040 and 878 cm−1, which represent hydrocarbon bond (-CH3 stretching vibration peak), amide bond (OC–N stretching vibration peak), and carbon-carbon double bond (=C–H bending vibration peak), respectively, belonging to the methylacrylamide group on the surface of CDs. The above results prove that methacrylamide groups has been successfully grafted on the surface of CDs.

The FTIR and 1H NMR of PMA14-CDs were provided in Figs. S3(Support information). The 1H NMR spectrum of the EVA resins was showed in Fig. S4. (Support information). The appearance of the synthesized nanohybrid PPD (PMA14-CD1%) was shown in Fig. S5 (Support information), which presented a brown colloidal state. At the same time, after adding 1500 ppm of PMA14-CD1%, the appearance and colour of diesel fuels hardly changed, indicating that CDs were well homo-dispersed in diesel fuels. The purchased EVA resins are a kind of industrial raw material with particle size of 3 mm–5 mm, which are shown in Fig. S6 (Support information). Four models of EVA resins were bought for testing, and their branched chains VA content (mol %) were 12%, 18%, 25% and 40%, which were named EVA12, EVA18, EVA25 and EVA40, respectively. Besides, EVA12 could not be dissolved in diesel fuels, while EVA18, EVA25 and EVA40 could be dissolved in diesel fuels within 15 min under ultrasound.

As shown in Fig. 5 , the peaks at 1020, 1240, 1739, 2854 and 2934 cm−1 corresponded to C–O–C stretching vibration peak, C–OH stretching vibration peak, ester group stretching vibration peak, –CH3 stretching vibration peak and –CH2- stretching vibration peak, respectively. In particular, the peak of the sample for EVA18, EVA25, EVA40 increases gradually at 1020 and 2854 cm−1, showing the increasing VA content in EVA resin.

As shown in Fig. 6 , the depression effects of diesel fuels were extensively affected by chain lengths of n-alkanes, the dosages of PPDs and the proportion of carbon dots in the PPDs. Typically, the design of PMA type PPDs follows the principle of carbon chain matching, that is, the carbon number of high-carbon ester as side chains in PMA should be the same as that of n-alkanes with most content in diesel fuel, so as to optimize the eutectic ability of PMA with paraffin. The carbon number of n-alkanes in this diesel is mainly distributed between 11 and 21, and the content of n-pentadecane is the most. According to the above principle, PPDs with tetradecyl and hexadecyl ester branched chains were used to measure the depressive effects of diesel fuel, and results showed that PMA14-CDs PPDs exhibited much better performance in both CFPP and SP reduction than that of PMA16-CD PPDs, which indicated that tetradecyl as branch of polymer match the paraffin and facilitate co-crystallize of them more effectively in diesel fuels. At the same time, with the increase dosages of PPDs in diesel fuels, both ΔCFPP and ΔSP first increased and subsequently tend to even. As the dose of PPDs added from 0 to 500 ppm, the ΔSP of all diesel samples treated with PPDs increases sharply, and diesel sample treated with PMA14-CD0.5% exhibited highest values. But for ΔCFPP improvement, only samples treated with PMA14-CD0.5% and PMA14-CD1% significantly changed with the increase of addition, while others changed less obvious, indicating that PMA14-CD0.5% PPD has the most effective improvement on ΔCFPP of diesel fuels, especially at low dose. Besides, the improvement of ΔCFPP and ΔSP were not obvious increase when the dose of PPDs is higher than 500 ppm, which demonstrated that the amount of PPDs eutectic with paraffin is close to saturation. In summary, for PMA14-CDs PPDs, the depressive effects is ranked: PMA14-CD0.5% > PMA14-CD1%>PMA14-CD0.25% > PMA14 > PMA14-CD2%, and PMA16-CDs PPDs exhibited similar effects order. Thus, the depressive effects on SP and CFPP of diesel fuel perform best when the addition amount of PMA is 1500 ppm, for ΔSP and ΔCFPP are 21 °C and 9 °C.

As shown in Fig. 7 , the 3D surface plot models on the cold flow properties of diesel fuel vary with the addition of PPDs and carbon dots proportion in PPDs are used to further analyse the contribution of these two factors. Among these, the factor X represents the proportion of carbon points in PPDs, and the factor Y represents the dosage of PPDs, Z axis represents the ΔCPFF or ΔSP average value. According to the results, the contribution of these two factors can be well fitted by mathematical model. Therefore, based on the statistical evaluation, the results show that ΔSP and ΔCFPP average values varied with the change of the proportion of carbon dots in PPDs and the amount of PPDs in diesel fuels, which can be fitted by parabolic model (1) and polynomial model (2) respectively, and they are expressed as follows:
(1) Z = - 1.3 + 9.5y - 2.6x2-4.7y2. Adj. R2 = 0.973, Prob > F = 0, F = 121.14
(2) Z = − 0.68 + 37.2x - 84.2x2 + 41.9x3+ 8.3x4 -6.5x5 + 0.1y. Adj. R2 = 0.861, Prob > F = 0, F = 938.34

The analysis of variance (ANOVA) of fitting models are given at the end of each equation. As shown, the F-value and significance value (P) are 121.14, 0 for Eq. (1) and 938.34, 0 for Eq. (2) respectively, for the value of the error probability (P) are both smaller than 0.01, which prove that these models are extremely significant. Besides, Adj. R2 value of Eq. (2) is closer to 1 than Eq. (1), which means the regression surface of ΔSP fits the observed values better than ΔCPFF. Overall, these characteristic parameters show that the fitting mathematical models are very suitable for predicting the ΔSP and ΔCFPP values of diesel fuel on various addition of PPDs and proportion of carbon dots in PPDs.

Based on the above, it can be inferred that the CDs can act as heterogeneous nucleation agent (core) of wax crystals and the alkyl branched chain serve as extranuclear micelles to help match the length of the paraffins in diesel fuels. The low proportion of CDs in PMA (PMA14-CD0.25% and PMA16-CD0.25%) leads to the low crosslinking degree of PMA. Thus, long-chains alkyl branched as extranuclear micelles may be oversized, resulting in large and irregular wax crystal growth. However, excessive CDs (nucleation sites) in PMA matrix (PMA14-CD2% and PMA16-CD2%) may lead the higher crosslinking of the polymer and bring about too short micelles on the surface of nucleation sites, in which paraffins can not effectively crystallize at the nucleation sites, resulting in poor performance in both CFPP and SP reduction.

In conclusion, combining appropriate amount of modified CDs with PMA through in situ free radical polymerization can effectively improve the cold flow properties of diesel fuels. PMA with 0.5% CDs show the increment of CFPP (5–8 °C) and SP (7–11 °C) than PMA14. It is worth noting that PMA14-CDs has an outstanding performance in reducing the solid point (SP) of diesel fuels. Finally, considering the economy and performance of PPDs, PMA14-CD0.5% is optimal in cold flow properties of diesel fuels, and the best effect of ΔSP and ΔCFPP are 21 °C and 9 °C at 1500 ppm.

Three kinds of EVA resin with different VA content were tested about the effect of the cold flow properties for diesel fuels. As shown in Fig. 8 , with the increase of EVA resins, the CFPP and SP curves change from rising to even, when the addition amount is less than 1000 ppm EVA resin could effectively reduce the CFPP and SP of diesel fuels. Diesel fuels treated with EVA25 showed best reduction effect of the CFPP for diesel fuels, and the ΔCFPP was - 13 °C at a dose of 1000 ppm. At the same time, diesel fuels treated with EVA 40 showed the best performance on reducing SP, in which ΔSP was - 13 °C at a dose of 1000 ppm. From the depressive effects of EVA resins on CFPP and SP of diesel fuels, it can be inferred that the polar groups on the surface of paraffin crystals increased with the increase of branched content in EVA, which could effectively prevent cross-linking between wax crystals, resulting the reducing of SP. However, excessive polar groups in paraffin crystals may lead to poor crystallinity, loose crystal structure and large size, so that the wax crystal was poor in fluidity and was not easy to pass through standardized wire mesh filter within a specified time. The appropriate amount of VA content (25%) in EVA is the most helpful to the improvement of CFPP, because it can not only make the wax crystal smaller and regular, but also effectively organize the cross-linking between wax crystals. As a result, EVA resin dissolved in diesel fuels improve the cold flow properties of diesel fuels, especially EVA25.

In order to further improve the cold flow performance of diesel fuels, combining technology was introduced. The effect of PMA14-C0.5%, EVA25 and their combination (mass ratio of 3:1, 2:1, 1:1, 1:2, 1:3) were showed in Fig. 9 , and the composite material were named PPD-1, PPD-2, PPD-3, PPD-4, and PPD-5, respectively, according to different mass ratio. As shown in Fig. 9, the combined PPDs show different depression effects of diesel fuel, indicating that the addition amount of PPDs and the composition of combined PPDs can affect the cold flow properties of diesel fuels significantly. Specifically, ΔCFPP and ΔSP of diesel fuels are gradually increased and subsequently tend to even with the increase of PPDs, when the dose of combined-PPDs is more than 1000 ppm, the improvement effect of the cold flow properties for diesel fuels becomes not significant. Adjusting the mass radio of PMA14-CD0.5% and EVA25 can obviously affect the depressive effect of combined PPDs. Specifically, the SP of diesel fuel can be greatly reduced by using PPDs with high proportion of PMA14-CD0.5% and low proportion of EVA25, eg. PPD-1 and PPD-2, while the depressive effect of PPD-1 on CFPP exhibited poor at low dose. On the other hand, the depressive effect of PPDs with low proportion of PMA14-CD0.5% and high proportion of EVA25 on SP are worse, which also account for the unsatisfactory depressive effects on CFPP. Plus, PPD-5 with low content of PMA14-CD0.5% show the measurement value of both ΔCFPP and ΔSP with increased error, indicating that its performance is unstable. As a result, it was found that when the mass ratio of PMA14-CD0.5% to EVA25 in combined PPD-3 is 1: 1, they show the greatest synergistic effect on reducing both CFPP and SP, in which the ΔCFPP and ΔSP reached the maximum of 20 °C and 30 °C at the dose of 1500 ppm.

Additionally, the 3D contour relationships between the proportion of PMA14-CD0.5% and addition amount of combined PPDs to the ΔCFPP and ΔSP value are described in Fig. 10 , for these two impacts can result in a significant reduction in the CFPP and SP of diesel fuels. Among them, the index X represents the proportion of PMA14-CD0.5% in combined PPDs, and the index Y represents the dosage of PPDs. Z axis represents the corresponding ΔCPFF or ΔSP average value. According to the statistical evaluation, it suggested that the poly (2D) model and polynomial model (2D) were found to best fitting the relationship among ΔSP (Z), the proportion of PMA14-CD0.5% (x) and addition amount of combined PPDs (y) (3), and relationship among ΔCFPP (Z), the proportion of PMA14-CD0.5% (x) and addition amount of combined PPDs (y) (4). Both of them can be expressed respectively as follows:
(3) Z = 991144.6–8925.8x + 28.6x2 - 0.04x3 + 72y – 3.2y2 + 0.07y3. Adj. R2 = 0.895, Prob > F = 6.95e−14, F = 241.0
(4) Z = −15.6 + 0.03x + 0.95y - 0.007y2. Adj. R2 = 0.957, Prob > F = 0, F = 1763.18

The analysis of variance (ANOVA) of fitting models are given at the end of each equation. Accordingly, the F-value and significance value (P) are 241 and 6.95e−14 for Eq. (3) and 1763.18 and 0 for Eq. (4) respectively, for the value of the error probability (P) both smaller than 0.01, which suggest that these models are extremely significant. Besides, Adj. R2 value of Eq. (4) is closer to 1 than Eq. (3), which indicate that the models are very suitable for predicting the ΔSP and ΔCFPP values of diesel fuel, and the fitting regression equation of ΔSP is more consistent with the observed values than that of ΔCPFF. As it can be seen from above, the combined PPDs made more significantly improvement of the cold flow properties for diesel fuels compared with pure PMA-CDs and EVA resin. It can be inferred that synergistic effect of PMA-CDs and EVA resin further improve the cold flow properties of diesel fuels. On one hand, PMA14-CD0.5% as an efficient nucleating agent can induce paraffin to nucleate around it, leading to the formation of fine crystals. On the other hands, EVA resin was eutectic with paraffin, resulting in polar groups covering the surface of wax crystals, so as to prevent the combination and reduce the friction among wax crystals. At last, the slope of each line chart of ΔCFPP and ΔSP in Fig. 9 corresponds to the degree to which the cold flow properties for diesel fuels varies with the dosage of combined PPDs. When the addition amount is greater than 1000 ppm, the slope of both ΔCFPP and ΔSP curves reached the minimum, indicating that the effect of combined PPDs on cold flow properties for diesel fuels was difficult to be promoted. Considering that the addition amount of PPDs should be as low as possible while maintaining a significant effect, 1000 ppm of PPD-2 is considered the most economical choice.

The microscope images of pure diesel fuels, diesel fuels added with PMA14-CD0.5%, EVA25 and PPD-2 in the temperature range from −5 °C to −40 °C were shown in Fig. 11 . When the temperature of pure diesel fuels drops slightly, a small amount of wax crystals form. As the temperature drops, there is almost no new wax crystal generation; instead, the size of the initially generated wax crystals significantly increases. These results show that the paraffin in diesel fuels oil follows the law of heterogeneous nucleation in the process of crystallisation at low temperature, and the initial wax crystal plays the role of nucleation sites. The morphology of wax crystals is close to spherical, and PMA-CD0.5% added in diesel fuels can be used as the core to nucleate paraffin. Furthermore, new small wax crystals continue to form as the temperature decreases, but the size of wax crystals does not change significantly.

Therefore, it can be inferred that PMA-CD0.5% effectively improves the nuclear barrier and lowers the surface energy of wax crystals, thereby inhibiting their growth. When EVA25 is dissolved in diesel fuels, it can cause paraffin to rapidly nucleate when the temperature initially decreases. Subsequently, when the temperature continues to fall, many new wax crystals are formed, and all wax crystals continue to grow longer in one direction to form rod-like crystals. Thus, it can be concluded that EVA25 exhibited better performance in reducing CFPP of diesel fuels than PMA-CD0.5% because it shows higher efficiency of promoting paraffin nucleation. Furthermore, PMA-CD0.5% outperformed EVA25 in terms of lowering diesel fuels CP, owing to its ability to significantly reduce wax crystal growth at a lower temperature. Finally, Fig. 11 (d1–d4) shows the effect of the combination of PMA14-CD0.5% and EVA25 (PPD-2). As the temperature initially falls, a large number of tiny wax crystals are generated. However, as the temperature drops, these tiny wax crystals do not grow any larger. Hence, PPD-2 fully combined the advantages of PMA-CD0.5% and EVA25 in improving the cold flow property of diesel fuels; it not only improves the nucleation rate of paraffin but also greatly limits the growth of wax crystals with the decrease in temperature. On diesel fuels, the synergistic effect of PMA-CD0.5% and EVA25 outperforms either of them individually.

As shown in Fig. 12
, when the temperature is −4.1 °C, −4 °C, −3,9 °C and −3.9 °C, the wax crystals of pure diesel fuels, diesel fuels added with PMA-CD0.5%, EVA25 and the combined PPD-2 are initially formed, respectively. Among them, diesel fuels added with PMA-CD0.5% shows a slight increase of 0.1 °C than pure diesel fuels, and diesel fuels added with EVA25 or PPD-2 shows a slight rise of 0.2 °C than pure diesel fuels. The phase transformation rate from paraffin to wax crystal is reflected by the slope on the right side of each curve's main peak value. The faster the transformation rate, the greater the slope value. It can be seen that the diesel fuels of phase conversion rate from fast to slow is a diesel fuels with EVA25, PPD-2, PMA14-CD0.5% and pure diesel fuels. These results indicate that the addition of PPDs to diesel fuels can improve the initial nucleation temperature of paraffin by increasing the rate of wax crystal generation during temperature reduction. In particular, these conclusions are consistent with the results of POM characterization. At the same time, the curves of PMA14-CD0.5% and PPD-2 both have a gentle and broad peak in the range from −25 °C to −40 °C, showing that phase transition energy is still released from diesel fuels. This shows that the addition of PMA-CD0.5% and PPD-2 to diesel fuels can effectively inhibit the conversion of paraffin into wax crystals at low temperature, consistent with the fact that these two PPDs can effectively reduce the SP of diesel fuels (see Figs. 9 and 13).

During the entire cooling process, the viscosity-reducing effect of PPDs is ranked: PPD-2 > PMA14-CD0.5% > EVA25. Among them, the viscosity of diesel fuels added PMA14-CD05% and diesel fuels added PPD-2 show almost no change until the temperature drops to −30 °C, which shows the excellent performance on reducing the viscosity of diesel fuels at a low temperature. In the temperature range from 0 °C to −15 °C, the diesel fuels added PMA14-CD0.5% and PPD-2 remain liquid and good fluidity, so their viscosity hasn't changed; the pure diesel fuels gradually solidifies and cause the viscosity increases; the liquid flow of diesel fuels added EVA-2 is gradually transformed into the mutual movement between the wax crystals due to the generation of large numbers of fine wax crystals, which lead the viscosity increases significantly. In the temperature range from −30 °C to −35 °C, the viscosity of diesel fuels with PMA14-CD0.5% is less than that of diesel fuels with PPD-2, which is also because the EVA25 contained in PPD-2 promotes massive crystallisation of paraffin.

To compare the effect of various PPDs in this kind of diesel fuels, we compare the effects of PPDs, which are produced by various corporations. The model and brand of these kinds of PPDs are shown in Table 1 .

Different brands of PPDs are used to test the improvement effect of the cold flow properties for diesel fuels, as shown in Fig. 14 . The dose of each PPD in diesel fuels is 1000 ppm. Among them, PPD-2, EVA and Hote-2 show excellent performance in reducing CFPP. In contrast, PPD-2, Hote-2 and HIRI-1122 show outstanding performance in reducing SP. PPD-2 (ΔCFPP = 19 °C) exhibits better performance than EVA (ΔCFPP = 10 °C) in reducing CFPP, and shows more depressive effects than Hote-2 on ΔSP (PPD-2, 29 °C; Hote-2, 19 °C). Besides, the effect of PPD-2, HOTE-2 and EVA on the cold flow properties of diesel fuels at different doses was shown in Fig. 15 . With the decrease of PPDs, the effect of PPDs on the cold flow properties of diesel fuels gradually become weak, while PPD-2 maintains the best performance in reducing CFPP and SP at any dosage.

A possible mechanism was proposed to explain the effect of PMA14-CDs, EVA resin and their combination on the nucleation of paraffin in diesel fuels and the growth of wax crystals at low temperatures, as shown in Fig. 16 . When the temperature of pure diesel fuels decreases, paraffin begins to crystallize and generates insufficient nucleation sites. Then, paraffin continues to deposit on the initial crystal nuclei, and gradually forms large-size flake wax crystals, which blocks standard filters. As the temperature decreases, the size and number of wax crystals increase, and finally stacking with each other form a 3D network structure to limit the flow of diesel fuels. In diesel fuels, PMA14-CDs acts as the nucleation site of paraffin, which can effectively make paraffin deposit on its surface and induce the generation of spherical wax crystals. Simultaneously, it greatly retards wax crystal growth and reduces stacking between wax crystals, lowering the SP. The EVA resin is a eutectic component of paraffin that can attach to the surface of wax crystals and orientate grow into a needle-like shape during paraffin crystallisation. Furthermore, due to its excellent eutectic ability, EVA resin can rapidly induce the generation of a large amount of nucleation. Then, EVA resin separates first from the liquid phase and promotes the precipitation of paraffin during cooling. During the cooling process, the combination of PMA14-CDs and EVA resin in diesel fuels not only promote the formation of spherical paraffin crystals but also inhibit the generation and growth of wax crystals, in which these fine and uniform spherical wax crystals replace flake-like or needle-like wax crystals in diesel fuels, improving its cold flow property.

To summarise, micellar CDs-PMA type nano-hybrid PPDs were synthesized by in-situ radical polymerization for the first time. Compared with linear PMA, this type of PPDs exhibited better performance in improving the cold flow properties of diesel fuels. Only 500 ppm of PMA14-CD0.5% was added to diesel fuels, the SP was reduced to 21 °C, while CFPP could also be reduced to 9 °C by adding more PPDs. Simultaneously, the effect of an EVA resin on improving the cold flow property of diesel fuels was examined. When diesel fuels contained 1000 ppm of EVA25, the CFPP of diesel fuels was reduced to 13 °C. Subsequently, the two kinds of PPDs were combined to treat diesel fuels, and combined PPDs showed outstanding performance in improving the cold flow property of diesel fuels. When the mass ratio of combined PPDs was 1:1 and the total addition was 1500 ppm, the SP and CFPP were reduced to 30 °C and 20 °C, respectively, which was better than many commercial PPDs. In addition, polynomial and quadratic function fitting models are established to describe how the two main factors, namely, the amount of PPD addition and carbon dots content in PPDs (1), and the proportion of PMA14-CD0.5% in combined PPDs and addition amount of combined PPDs (2) affect the cold flow properties of diesel fuels, respectively, and the fitting effects are both well. Finally, a new mechanism was proposed about how PMA14-CDs, EVA resin and their combined effect on the heterogeneous nucleation and crystallisation behaviour of paraffin is proposed.

Xiaokang Zhang: Investigation, Data curation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Nana Li: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Zhong Wei: Investigation, Validation, Methodology. Sheng Han: Methodology, Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Bin Dai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Supervision. Hualin Lin: Resources, Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Project Number 22008155, 22075183, 21878188 and 21975161), Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality Project (Project Number 18090503800), Shuguang Program of Shanghai Education Development Foundation and Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (Project Number 18SG52), Chenguang Program of Shanghai Education Development Foundation and Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (Project Number 19CG69), the Talent Development Foundation of Shanghai (Project Number 2018034), Shanghai Gaofeng & Gaoyuan Project for University Academic Program Development.

Fuel

Energy Fuels
